summary: The main claim is: For example, it might follow the model of the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN).
International cooperation to mitigate the risks of AI development will require creating new solutions.
The main claim is: During training, you use (3kmp^2/N) FLOP/s, and at inference you can run one model for kp FLOP/s.
So whenever we finish end-to-end training a language model, we can run a lot of them in parallel.
The main claim is: This is not a study, more like trying to tinker with the prompt to make it consistently work; but I think studying such prompts in a more consistent way may be a good idea.

It appears that right now it is very hard to make a small prompt that will accomplish this; I think it is worthwhile to describe the whole process for designing it, as well as presenting common failure modes.
The main claim is: Saying that AI transcending human-level intelligence is a relevant threshold may sound to people as if the real danger is AIs developing consciousness.

The reason for this is that the lack of concreteness about what human intelligence is could invite people to substitute what they believe exists in everyone's brain, regardless of their intelligence, and that is consciousness.

The main claim is: I think it's actively unhelpful to talk about P(doom) because it fails to distinguish between literally everyone dying and humanity failing to capture 99.99% of the value of the future under a total utilitarian view but in practice, everyo…
The main claim is: Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models (Perez et al., 2022).
Fine-tunes a model on its own "high confidence" outputs.
The main claim is: I think reflectivity happens earlier then you might think in embedded RL agents.
Because of these things I think we can get useful reflectivity and study it in sub-dangerous AI.
The main claim is: Just like every Monday now, researchers in AI Alignment are invited for a coffee time, to talk about their research and what they're into.
Small change for this second edition: the link to the walled garden now only works for AF members.
The main claim is: Models of AI Safety ------------------- The reason I think this is bad, is that I think it invalidates my ideas about how AI Safety could be implemented in the real world.
Probability I think any other team would impose such a plan: ~30%) 1.
The main claim is: The subcortex has an incoming signal that tells it that the neocortex is imagining / expecting / remembering the taste of salt.
Now we get a crucial ingredient: I hypothesize that the subcortex somehow knows when the neocortex is imagining the taste of salt.
